facts.text: The Phantom of the Opera is the longest-running show in Broadway history, playing 13,981 performances over its 35-year run from 1988 to 2023.
Hamilton creator Lin-Manuel Miranda was inspired to write the musical after reading Ron Chernow's biography of Alexander Hamilton while on vacation in 2008.
The Tony Awards are named after Antoinette Perry, a director and co-founder of the American Theatre Wing, who passed away in 1946.
Wicked, which opened on Broadway in 2003, is based on Gregory Maguire's 1995 novel reimagining the witches of Oz, not directly on L. Frank Baum's original stories.
Les Misérables originally premiered in Paris in 1980 as a French-language concept album before becoming a stage musical and transferring to London and then Broadway.
The first Tony Awards ceremony was held on April 6, 1947, at the Waldorf-Astoria Hotel in New York City, with no television broadcast.
West Side Story, which reimagined Romeo and Juliet in 1950s New York, was a collaboration between Leonard Bernstein, Stephen Sondheim, Arthur Laurents, and choreographer Jerome Robbins.
A Chorus Line held the record as Broadway's longest-running show for 15 years, from 1983 to 1997, with 6,137 performances.
Chicago originally opened in 1975 but became a much bigger hit in its 1996 revival, which is now the longest-running American musical in Broadway history.
Rent, Jonathan Larson's rock musical loosely based on Puccini's La Bohème, opened off-Broadway the night after Larson died of an aortic dissection at age 35.
The Lion King musical uses over 232 puppets and has grossed more than any entertainment title in box office history, surpassing $1 billion on Broadway alone.
Cats, based on T.S. Eliot's Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats, was the first musical to surpass 7,000 performances on Broadway when it closed in 2000.
Fiddler on the Roof was the first musical to surpass 3,000 performances on Broadway, running from 1964 to 1972 with 3,242 shows.
My Fair Lady, based on George Bernard Shaw's Pygmalion, opened in 1956 and held the record as the longest-running Broadway musical for nearly a decade.
Oklahoma!, which premiered in 1943, is widely considered the first fully integrated musical where songs and dances advanced the plot rather than serving as standalone entertainment.
The Sound of Music was the final collaboration between Richard Rodgers and Oscar Hammerstein II; Hammerstein died of cancer just nine months after its Broadway premiere.
South Pacific tackled racial prejudice through the song 'You've Got to Be Carefully Taught,' which was considered so controversial that legislators in Georgia tried to ban it.
Guys and Dolls, often called the perfect musical comedy, is based on the short stories and colorful characters of Damon Runyon set in Times Square.
Hello, Dolly! starring Carol Channing won a then-record 10 Tony Awards in 1964, a record that stood until The Producers won 12 in 2001.
Grease originally opened off-Broadway in 1972 before transferring to Broadway, where it ran for 3,388 performances and became the decade's longest-running show.
Cabaret, set in 1931 Berlin, was one of the first musicals to use its setting and songs as political commentary on the rise of Nazism.
Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street is considered Stephen Sondheim's masterpiece, blending Grand Guignol horror with operatic musical storytelling.
Into the Woods interweaves several Brothers Grimm fairy tales and uses them as metaphors for the consequences of getting what you wish for.
Company, Stephen Sondheim's 1970 musical about a single man and his married friends, was revolutionary for having no linear plot, focusing instead on a concept.
A Little Night Music features the only Sondheim song to become a genuine pop hit: 'Send in the Clowns,' recorded by artists from Frank Sinatra to Judy Collins.
Sunday in the Park with George was inspired by Georges Seurat's painting A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte and won the 1985 Pulitzer Prize for Drama.
Follies, Sondheim's 1971 musical about aging showgirls reuniting, originally cost $800,000 to produce — an enormous sum at the time — and ran at a loss despite strong reviews.
Gypsy, based on the memoirs of burlesque performer Gypsy Rose Lee, is often called the greatest American musical ever written despite never winning the Tony for Best Musical.
Annie, the musical based on the Little Orphan Annie comic strip, almost didn't make it to Broadway; producer Mike Nichols initially passed on the project.
The Music Man was written by Meredith Willson, who spent eight years and over 30 drafts perfecting the show before it opened on Broadway in 1957.
Kiss Me, Kate, Cole Porter's musical-within-a-musical based on The Taming of the Shrew, won the first-ever Tony Award for Best Musical in 1949.
The King and I starring Yul Brynner opened in 1951; Brynner would go on to play the King of Siam over 4,600 times on stage throughout his career.
Carousel, Rodgers and Hammerstein's 1945 musical, was named the best musical of the 20th century by Time magazine in 1999.
Show Boat, which premiered in 1927, is considered the first true musical play, tackling serious themes of racial injustice and miscegenation.
Porgy and Bess, George Gershwin's folk opera set in Charleston, South Carolina, features the immortal standard 'Summertime,' one of the most recorded songs in history.
42nd Street was adapted from the 1933 film and opened on Broadway in 1980, becoming famous for producer David Merrick's announcement of director Gower Champion's death on opening night.
Dreamgirls, loosely based on the story of The Supremes, introduced Jennifer Holliday, whose showstopping performance of 'And I Am Telling You I'm Not Going' became legendary.
Evita, Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice's musical about Eva Perón, made Patti LuPone a Broadway star when it opened in 1979.
Jesus Christ Superstar started as a concept album in 1970 before becoming a stage production, making it one of the first rock operas to achieve mainstream success.
Godspell, based on the Gospel of Matthew, was Stephen Schwartz's first produced musical; he was just 23 years old when it premiered off-Broadway in 1971.
Hair, the tribal love-rock musical, shocked audiences in 1968 with its nudity, drug references, and anti-Vietnam War message, becoming the definitive counterculture musical.
Pippin, directed and choreographed by Bob Fosse for its original 1972 production, was one of the first Broadway shows to be marketed through television commercials.
The Producers won a record 12 Tony Awards in 2001, breaking the record of 10 previously held by Hello, Dolly! and surpassed only by itself.
Spamalot, based on Monty Python and the Holy Grail, features the song 'The Song That Goes Like This,' a parody of overwrought Broadway power ballads.
The Book of Mormon, created by Trey Parker, Matt Stone, and Robert Lopez, won nine Tony Awards in 2011 and recouped its $11.4 million investment in just nine months.
Dear Evan Hansen explores teenage anxiety and social media culture; Ben Platt originated the title role and won the 2017 Tony for Best Actor in a Musical.
Come From Away tells the true story of passengers stranded in Gander, Newfoundland, after the September 11, 2001, attacks and features a cast of just 12 playing multiple roles.
Hadestown, Anaïs Mitchell's folk-opera retelling of the Orpheus and Eurydice myth, began as a community theater project in Vermont before reaching Broadway in 2019.
Six, the pop musical about Henry VIII's six wives, originated as a student project at the Edinburgh Fringe Festival before becoming a West End and Broadway sensation.
Moulin Rouge! The Musical uses a jukebox format with over 70 songs from artists spanning decades, from Offenbach to Beyoncé.
Beetlejuice was closed to make room for The Music Man revival starring Hugh Jackman, but fan protests and strong ticket sales brought it back to a different theater.
Mean Girls, with music by Jeff Richmond and lyrics by Nell Benjamin, was adapted from Tina Fey's hit 2004 film and opened on Broadway in 2018.
Waitress made history in 2016 as the first Broadway musical with an all-female creative team: songwriter Sara Bareilles, writer Jessie Nelson, director Diane Paulus, and choreographer Lorin Latarro.
Beautiful: The Carole King Musical chronicles the songwriter's rise from teenager writing hits in the Brill Building to the recording of her landmark album Tapestry.
Jersey Boys tells the story of Frankie Valli and the Four Seasons and won the 2006 Tony for Best Musical despite being considered an underdog against The Color Purple.
Mamma Mia!, built around the songs of ABBA, has been seen by over 65 million people worldwide and spawned two hit films starring Meryl Streep.
Kinky Boots, featuring songs by Cyndi Lauper, made her the first woman to win the Tony Award for Best Original Score by herself when it triumphed in 2013.
Newsies, based on the 1992 Disney film about the 1899 newsboys' strike, became a surprise Broadway hit in 2012 after a successful run at the Paper Mill Playhouse.
Aladdin has been running on Broadway since 2014, and the Genie role originally played by James Monroe Iglehart has become one of Broadway's most coveted comedic parts.
Frozen on Broadway expanded the film's score with several new songs by Kristen Anderson-Lopez and Robert Lopez, though it closed earlier than expected in 2020.
The Little Mermaid musical ran on Broadway from 2008 to 2009 and was notable for its innovative staging of underwater movement using Heelys roller shoes.
Mary Poppins on Broadway used innovative stage technology to make the title character appear to fly over the audience in the 'Step in Time' sequence.
Beauty and the Beast was Disney's first stage musical adaptation, opening on Broadway in 1994 and running for over 5,400 performances across 13 years.
Miss Saigon is a modern retelling of Puccini's Madame Butterfly set during the Vietnam War, and its original London production featured a real helicopter landing on stage.
Starlight Express, Andrew Lloyd Webber's roller-skating musical about trains, is one of the most commercially successful musicals ever despite receiving poor critical reviews.
Joseph and the Amazing Technicolor Dreamcoat was the first collaboration between Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice, originally written as a 15-minute cantata for a school choir in 1968.
Avenue Q used puppets alongside human actors to tackle adult themes, winning the 2004 Tony for Best Musical in an upset over Wicked.
Spring Awakening, based on Frank Wedekind's 1891 play about teenage sexuality, was a breakthrough for director Michael Mayer and songwriter Duncan Sheik.
Next to Normal was the first rock musical to win the Pulitzer Prize for Drama, exploring a mother's struggle with bipolar disorder.
Fun Home, based on Alison Bechdel's graphic memoir, was the first Broadway musical with a lesbian protagonist to win the Tony for Best Musical in 2015.
Once, based on the 2007 Irish film, was unique on Broadway for having its actors play their own instruments live on stage throughout the entire show.
In the Heights, Lin-Manuel Miranda's first Broadway musical, won four Tony Awards in 2008 and is set in the largely Dominican-American neighborhood of Washington Heights.
Urinetown is a satirical musical about a dystopia where private toilets are banned, and it won three Tony Awards in 2002 including Best Book and Best Original Score.
Thoroughly Modern Millie won the 2002 Tony for Best Musical and starred Sutton Foster, launching her career as one of Broadway's biggest leading ladies.
Hairspray won eight Tony Awards in 2003, and Harvey Fierstein won for Best Book of a Musical as well as originating the role of Edna Turnblad in drag.
The Color Purple, based on Alice Walker's Pulitzer Prize-winning novel, had a 2015 revival starring Cynthia Erivo that was hailed as even better than the original.
Memphis, set in the 1950s, explores the birth of rock and roll and racial integration in the music scene, winning the 2010 Tony for Best Musical.
Tootsie, the musical adaptation of the 1982 Dustin Hoffman film, won the 2019 Tony for Best Book of a Musical for Robert Horn's screenplay adaptation.
Mrs. Doubtfire, the musical based on the Robin Williams film, opened on Broadway in 2021 and was praised for Rob McClure's physical comedy in the title role.
Back to the Future: The Musical transferred from London's West End to Broadway in 2023, featuring a DeLorean time machine that appears to fly into the audience.
MJ The Musical, a jukebox musical about Michael Jackson, won four Tony Awards in 2022, with Myles Frost winning Best Actor for his portrayal of the pop icon.
& Juliet reimagines what would happen if Shakespeare's Juliet decided not to die, set to a jukebox score of hit songs by pop songwriter Max Martin.
Death of a Salesman by Arthur Miller premiered in 1949 and won the Tony for Best Play, the Pulitzer Prize, and the New York Drama Critics Circle Award in the same year.
A Streetcar Named Desire launched Marlon Brando to stardom in 1947; his raw, Method-acting portrayal of Stanley Kowalski changed American theater forever.
Long Day's Journey Into Night, Eugene O'Neill's autobiographical masterpiece, was written in 1941 but not produced until 1956, three years after his death, per his wishes.
Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf? by Edward Albee was denied the 1963 Pulitzer Prize when the advisory board overruled the drama jury, finding the play too controversial.
Angels in America: Millennium Approaches won the 1993 Tony for Best Play and the Pulitzer Prize, while Part Two: Perestroika won the Best Play Tony the following year.
The Glass Menagerie, Tennessee Williams' memory play, launched his career in 1945 and established the use of a narrator character looking back on past events.
Our Town by Thornton Wilder, first performed in 1938, is deliberately performed with minimal sets and props to emphasize universal themes of life and death.
The Crucible, Arthur Miller's 1953 allegory about the Salem witch trials, was written as a commentary on McCarthyism and the Red Scare of the 1950s.
Twelve Angry Men by Reginald Rose was originally a 1954 teleplay before becoming a classic stage play; the jury deliberation drama takes place in a single room in real time.
Inherit the Wind, inspired by the 1925 Scopes Monkey Trial, premiered on Broadway in 1955 and has been revived multiple times as debates about science and religion persist.
Amadeus by Peter Shaffer dramatizes the rivalry between Mozart and Salieri and won the 1981 Tony for Best Play, with Ian McKellen in the original Broadway cast.
Equus by Peter Shaffer explores a psychiatrist's investigation of a boy who blinds six horses; the play requires actors to wear stylized horse head structures designed by John Napier.
Art by Yasmina Reza, about three friends arguing over a white painting, won the 1998 Tony for Best Play and starred Alan Alda, Victor Garber, and Alfred Molina.
The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-Time won five Tony Awards in 2015, praised for its innovative staging that used LED lights embedded in the set floor.
Stephen Sondheim wrote the lyrics for both West Side Story and Gypsy before age 30, then went on to compose and write lyrics for over a dozen groundbreaking musicals.
Richard Rodgers is the only person to have won all five major entertainment awards: an Emmy, a Grammy, an Oscar, a Tony, and a Pulitzer Prize — the original EGOT-plus.
Oscar Hammerstein II revolutionized musical theater by insisting that songs emerge naturally from character and story, a principle he applied from Show Boat through The Sound of Music.
Andrew Lloyd Webber has had six musicals running simultaneously in London's West End, a feat unmatched by any other composer in theater history.
Lin-Manuel Miranda is one of the few people to achieve EGOT status, completing it with his Emmy win in 2024 after already earning his Grammy, Oscar, and Tony.
Stephen Schwartz wrote the scores for Godspell, Pippin, and Wicked, as well as the songs for the Disney films Pocahontas, The Hunchback of Notre Dame, and Enchanted.
Kander and Ebb, the songwriting team behind Cabaret, Chicago, and Kiss of the Spider Woman, collaborated for over 40 years until Fred Ebb's death in 2004.
Cole Porter wrote both music and lyrics for his shows and continued writing after a devastating 1937 horse riding accident that nearly cost him both legs.
Irving Berlin, who wrote over 1,500 songs including 'God Bless America' and 'White Christmas,' was a self-taught musician who could only play piano in the key of F-sharp.
Bob Fosse's distinctive choreographic style — turned-in knees, rolled shoulders, bowler hats, and jazz hands — became one of the most recognizable aesthetics in theater history.
Jerome Robbins directed and choreographed both the stage and film versions of West Side Story; his ballet-influenced choreography changed how dance was used in musicals.
Harold Prince directed more Tony Award-winning Best Musicals than any other director, including Cabaret, Company, Follies, A Little Night Music, Sweeney Todd, Evita, and Phantom.
Julie Taymor became the first woman to win the Tony for Best Direction of a Musical for The Lion King in 1998; her innovative puppetry designs also won Best Costume Design.
Susan Stroman directed and choreographed The Producers, winning Tony Awards for both roles in 2001, and also directed The Scottsboro Boys and Bullets Over Broadway.
Audra McDonald holds the record for most Tony Awards won by any performer, with six competitive wins across four different acting categories.
Bernadette Peters has been one of Broadway's biggest stars for five decades, originating roles in Sunday in the Park with George, Into the Woods, and Song and Dance.
Ethel Merman, known for her powerful belt voice that needed no microphone, originated roles in Annie Get Your Gun, Gypsy, and Call Me Madam.
Patti LuPone originated the role of Eva Perón in the Broadway production of Evita in 1979 and won her second Tony for Best Actress in a Musical for Gypsy in 2008.
Nathan Lane has won three Tony Awards and is beloved for originating the role of Max Bialystock in The Producers opposite Matthew Broderick.
Chita Rivera was the first Hispanic woman to receive the Kennedy Center Honor and originated roles in West Side Story, Chicago, and Kiss of the Spider Woman.
Zero Mostel originated three iconic Broadway roles: Pseudolus in A Funny Thing Happened on the Way to the Forum, Tevye in Fiddler on the Roof, and Max Bialystock in The Producers.
Mandy Patinkin won the Tony for Best Actor in a Musical for Evita in 1980 and later originated the role of Georges Seurat in Sunday in the Park with George.
Betty Buckley won the Tony for Best Featured Actress in a Musical for Cats in 1983, performing 'Memory' so powerfully that Andrew Lloyd Webber called it definitive.
Carol Channing originated the role of Dolly Levi in Hello, Dolly! in 1964 and became so associated with the part that she played it over 5,000 times throughout her career.
Idina Menzel originated the role of Elphaba in Wicked and Maureen in Rent, making her one of the few performers to originate two iconic Broadway roles.
Kristin Chenoweth won the Tony for her role as Sally in You're a Good Man, Charlie Brown and then originated the role of Glinda in Wicked.
Ben Vereen won the Tony for Best Actor in a Musical for Pippin in 1973, becoming a major crossover star in theater, television, and film.
Angela Lansbury won five Tony Awards over a career spanning from Mame in 1966 to her final Broadway appearance in a 2009 revival of Blithe Spirit.
Joel Grey won the Tony for Best Featured Actor in a Musical for Cabaret in 1967 and recreated his role as the Emcee in the 1972 film, winning the Academy Award.
Barbara Streisand's Broadway career launched with I Can Get It for You Wholesale in 1962 and her Tony-nominated star turn in Funny Girl in 1964.
The Broadway theater district in Manhattan encompasses roughly 41 professional theaters, most clustered around Times Square between 41st and 54th Streets.
Broadway theaters must have 500 or more seats to be classified as 'Broadway'; smaller venues between 100 and 499 seats are considered 'Off-Broadway.'
The Shubert Organization is the largest theater owner on Broadway, operating 17 theaters, and was founded by three brothers from Syracuse, New York, in the early 1900s.
The Nederlander Organization is the second-largest Broadway theater owner, operating nine Broadway houses, and is run by the Nederlander family.
Jujamcyn Theaters, now known as Ambassador Theatre Group, owns five Broadway theaters including the St. James, where Oklahoma! and The Producers both premiered.
The longest-running show in Broadway history is The Phantom of the Opera (1988–2023) with 13,981 performances, followed by Chicago (1996–present).
The shortest-running Broadway show is believed to be The Intimate Revue in 1930, which closed during its opening night intermission after just one act.
The TKTS booth in Times Square, run by the Theatre Development Fund, has been selling discounted same-day Broadway tickets since 1973.
The Gypsy Robe is a Broadway tradition where an ensemble member who has the most Broadway chorus credits receives a decorated robe on opening night for good luck.
The term 'break a leg' as a good-luck wish in theater may derive from the superstition that wishing someone good luck would actually bring bad luck.
A Broadway ghost light — a single bare bulb left burning on stage when the theater is empty — is both a safety measure and a theatrical superstition.
Saying 'Macbeth' inside a theater is considered extremely bad luck; actors refer to it as 'The Scottish Play' to avoid the supposed curse.
The phrase 'the show must go on' is one of the oldest traditions in theater, emphasizing that performances continue regardless of backstage difficulties.
Broadway's first electric marquee appeared in 1880, and by the early 1900s, the theater district was already known as 'The Great White Way' for its dazzling lights.
The Tony Award trophy is a medallion designed by art director Herman Rosse, depicting the masks of comedy and tragedy on one side and a profile of Antoinette Perry on the other.
The 1998 Tony Awards ceremony is remembered for the live performance of 'The Ragtime Medley' and the competitive race between The Lion King and Ragtime.
Hamilton won 11 Tony Awards in 2016, tying the record previously held by The Producers, though it lost Best Actor to Leslie Odom Jr.'s co-star nomination confusion — Odom won.
The Tonys have been broadcast on television since 1967, making it one of the oldest award show telecasts still airing.
In 1976, A Chorus Line won nine Tony Awards, sweeping nearly every category it was nominated for and winning the Pulitzer Prize for Drama.
The Tony Award for Best Musical has been given since 1949, when Kiss Me, Kate became the first recipient.
The Color Purple won zero Tonys in 2006 despite 11 nominations, making it one of the most nominated shows to go home empty-handed.
The 2020 Tony Awards were delayed to September 2020 and then further to September 2021 due to COVID-19, with Broadway fully shutting down for 18 months.
Hamilton's original Broadway cast recording debuted at No. 12 on the Billboard 200, the highest chart position for a cast recording since 1963.
The original cast of Rent included Taye Diggs, Idina Menzel, Adam Pascal, Anthony Rapp, Jesse L. Martin, and Daphne Rubin-Vega.
Les Misérables is based on Victor Hugo's 1862 novel; the musical features the iconic barricade scene and the songs 'One Day More,' 'I Dreamed a Dream,' and 'On My Own.'
The Phantom of the Opera's chandelier crash is one of Broadway's most spectacular stage effects, with a crystal chandelier swinging from the ceiling into the audience area.
Wicked's 'Defying Gravity,' the Act One finale, became an anthem of empowerment and is regularly cited as one of the greatest Broadway songs ever written.
Hamilton popularized the use of hip-hop and R&B in musical theater and cast non-white actors as the Founding Fathers, deliberately challenging historical narratives.
West Side Story's 'Tonight Quintet' weaves five vocal parts together into a single ensemble number, considered one of the most complex pieces in musical theater.
The Phantom of the Opera's famous line 'The Phantom of the Opera is there, inside my mind' opens the show's iconic title song duet between the Phantom and Christine.
My Fair Lady's 'The Rain in Spain' — where Eliza finally speaks properly — is one of Broadway's most joyful moments, with Rex Harrison and Julie Andrews in the original cast.
Oklahoma!'s opening number, 'Oh, What a Beautiful Mornin',' broke convention by having a solo character sing onstage with no chorus line, setting a pastoral tone.
The Sound of Music introduced the world to 'Do-Re-Mi,' 'My Favorite Things,' 'Edelweiss,' and 'Climb Ev'ry Mountain,' all becoming enduring standards.
South Pacific's 'Some Enchanted Evening' became one of the best-selling singles of 1949, recorded by Perry Como, Bing Crosby, Frank Sinatra, and others.
Guys and Dolls features the comic masterpiece 'Sit Down, You're Rockin' the Boat,' a revival-meeting number that brings down the house in every production.
Hello, Dolly!'s title song was so popular that Louis Armstrong's recording knocked the Beatles off the No. 1 spot on the Billboard Hot 100 in May 1964.
Chicago's 'All That Jazz' opens the show with a signature Bob Fosse-style number that sets the tone for the entire cynical, vaudevillian musical.
Cabaret's 'Willkommen' introduces the audience to the Kit Kat Klub and the mysterious Emcee, one of the most enigmatic characters in musical theater.
Sweeney Todd's 'A Little Priest' is a darkly comic duet where Todd and Mrs. Lovett plan to bake murder victims into meat pies, listing various professions as flavors.
Into the Woods' 'No One Is Alone' is a tender reassurance sung to frightened characters that has become one of Sondheim's most performed concert pieces.
Company's 'Being Alive,' Bobby's final number, was rewritten multiple times during previews; Sondheim initially wrote 'Happily Ever After' but Dean Jones found it too cynical.
Gypsy's 'Everything's Coming Up Roses' is considered one of the greatest curtain-dropping Act One finales in musical theater history.
Annie's 'Tomorrow' became one of the most recognizable Broadway songs ever; Andrea McArdle originated the role at age 13.
The Music Man's 'Trouble' speech-song in River City was performed by Robert Preston, who rattled off rapid-fire lyrics about a pool table corrupting youth.
The King and I's 'Shall We Dance?' polka scene between Anna and the King is one of Broadway's most magical romantic moments.
Carousel's 'You'll Never Walk Alone' became the anthem of Liverpool Football Club and is sung by their fans before every home match at Anfield.
Show Boat's 'Ol' Man River' is one of the most powerful songs in musical theater, originally performed by Paul Robeson and addressing the suffering of Black dock workers.
Rent's 'Seasons of Love' asks the audience to measure a year in 525,600 minutes of love, becoming the show's signature anthem.
The Producers features 'Springtime for Hitler,' an intentionally offensive musical number that is the centerpiece of the show-within-a-show.
The Book of Mormon's 'I Believe' is a hilarious showstopper where Elder Price professes increasingly absurd articles of faith with complete sincerity.
In the Heights' 'In the Heights' opening number introduces the vibrant Washington Heights community through rap, salsa, and traditional musical theater forms.
Les Misérables' 'I Dreamed a Dream,' sung by Fantine, became a global phenomenon in 2009 when Susan Boyle performed it on Britain's Got Talent.
The Lion King's 'Circle of Life' opening number, with its stunning puppet procession down the theater aisles, is considered one of Broadway's greatest openings.
Hadestown's 'Wait for Me' features Orpheus's descent to the underworld accompanied by the show's onstage band, the Fates, and innovative staging.
Six reimagines Catherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, Jane Seymour, Anna of Cleves, Katherine Howard, and Catherine Parr as modern pop stars in a concert competition.
Moulin Rouge!'s 'El Tango de Roxanne' mashes up The Police's 'Roxanne' with tango choreography in one of the show's most dramatic sequences.
Dear Evan Hansen's 'Waving Through a Window' captures the isolation of social anxiety and became a viral hit even before the show opened on Broadway.
Come From Away's entire story unfolds on a simple set of chairs and tables that the cast rearranges to create different locations, reflecting the show's theme of community.
Miss Saigon's helicopter scene, depicting the fall of Saigon, was so technically complex that it required its own dedicated crew member to operate nightly.
Avenue Q's 'The Internet Is for Porn' became a viral hit, demonstrating Broadway's ability to satirize modern life with puppet comedy.
Spring Awakening's 'The Bitch of Living' shocked audiences with its raw depiction of teenage sexual frustration set to alt-rock music.
Fun Home's 'Ring of Keys,' sung by the young Alison, captures the moment a child recognizes herself in a butch woman at a diner — a landmark moment for LGBTQ+ representation on Broadway.
David Merrick, known as 'The Abominable Showman,' was one of Broadway's most powerful and controversial producers, behind Hello, Dolly!, 42nd Street, and Gypsy.
Cameron Mackintosh produced the four mega-musicals of the 1980s: Les Misérables, The Phantom of the Opera, Cats, and Miss Saigon, transforming global musical theater.
The Shubert Alley, a private pedestrian walkway between 44th and 45th Streets, is a famous gathering spot for theatergoers and has been a part of Broadway culture since the early 1900s.
The Palace Theatre on Broadway, opened in 1913, was considered the most prestigious vaudeville house in America and has hosted shows from Sweet Charity to SpongeBob SquarePants.
The Majestic Theatre, which housed The Phantom of the Opera for its entire 35-year run, has 1,681 seats and is located on West 44th Street.
The Winter Garden Theatre hosted Cats for its entire original Broadway run of 18 years and was extensively remodeled for the production.
George M. Cohan, known as the father of American musical theater, wrote 'Give My Regards to Broadway' and 'I'm a Yankee Doodle Dandy' in the early 1900s.
Florenz Ziegfeld's Follies, produced annually from 1907 to 1931, were lavish revues that set the standard for Broadway spectacle and introduced stars like Fanny Brice.
The Great Depression nearly destroyed Broadway, reducing the number of new productions from 264 in the 1927–28 season to just 98 in the 1930–31 season.
Broadway went dark on September 11, 2001, and all theaters closed for two days; it was only the third unscheduled closure in Broadway history.
Broadway's longest shutdown in modern history was the 18-month closure from March 2020 to September 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
EGOT stands for Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony; notable EGOT winners who completed the feat through a Tony include Audrey Hepburn, Whoopi Goldberg, and Mel Brooks.
Rita Moreno became an EGOT winner after adding her Tony to a career that included an Oscar for West Side Story, a Grammy, and multiple Emmy Awards.
John Gielgud, one of the greatest stage actors of the 20th century, won a Tony Award for Best Director for Big Fish, Little Fish in 1961, decades before his EGOT-completing Oscar.
Robert Lopez is the youngest EGOT winner and the only person to win all four awards twice, earning Tonys for Avenue Q and The Book of Mormon.
The 'Triple Crown of Acting' — winning an Oscar, Emmy, and Tony — has been achieved by performers including Helen Mirren, Jessica Lange, and Al Pacino.
Julie Andrews was passed over for the My Fair Lady film in favor of Audrey Hepburn, but she won the Oscar that year for Mary Poppins instead.
Mary Martin originated the roles of Nellie Forbush in South Pacific and Maria von Trapp in The Sound of Music, becoming one of Broadway's most beloved leading ladies.
Liza Minnelli won the Tony for Best Actress in a Musical for Flora the Red Menace in 1965 at age 19, making her the youngest Tony winner in that category at the time.
James Earl Jones won two Tony Awards during his career and was known for his commanding stage presence in The Great White Hope, Fences, and other plays.
The original Broadway production of A Raisin in the Sun in 1959, starring Sidney Poitier, was the first play by a Black woman — Lorraine Hansberry — to be produced on Broadway.
August Wilson wrote a cycle of ten plays, one set in each decade of the 20th century, collectively known as the Pittsburgh Cycle or the American Century Cycle.
Fences by August Wilson won the 1987 Pulitzer Prize and Tony for Best Play; its 2010 revival starring Denzel Washington won the Tony for Best Revival of a Play.
Tony Kushner's Angels in America is a two-part, seven-hour epic about the AIDS crisis in 1980s America and is considered one of the most important American plays ever written.
Edward Albee's Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf? features a brutal evening of psychological warfare between a married couple, George and Martha, and their younger guests.
Arthur Miller and Tennessee Williams are often paired as the two greatest American playwrights of the 20th century, representing realism and poetic expressionism, respectively.
Eugene O'Neill won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1936 and is the only American playwright to receive the honor.
Neil Simon was Broadway's most commercially successful playwright, with hits including Barefoot in the Park, The Odd Couple, Brighton Beach Memoirs, and Lost in Yonkers.
Tom Stoppard's Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead reimagines Hamlet from the perspective of two minor characters, blending existentialist philosophy with absurdist comedy.
David Mamet's Glengarry Glen Ross won the 1984 Pulitzer Prize for Drama, and its profanity-laden dialogue about desperate real estate salesmen became instantly iconic.
The original Broadway cast of Cats included Betty Buckley, Ken Page, and Terrence Mann; the show ran from 1982 to 2000 with 7,485 performances.
Fiddler on the Roof's 'Tradition' opening number introduces the audience to the customs and social structure of the Jewish shtetl of Anatevka in Tsarist Russia.
Zero Mostel's performance as Tevye in Fiddler on the Roof was so acclaimed that Harvey Fierstein, Chaim Topol, and many others have since made the role their own.
Oklahoma! was the first Rodgers and Hammerstein musical and ran for 2,212 performances, a Broadway record at the time, and pioneered the integrated book musical format.
The Tony Award for Best Revival of a Musical was first given in 1994, with a retroactive special award for the 1992 revival of Guys and Dolls.
Angela Lansbury won the Tony for Best Actress in a Musical for Mame in 1966, Dear World in 1969, Gypsy in 1975, and Sweeney Todd in 1979.
Harvey Fierstein is the first openly gay person to win a Tony Award, winning for Best Play and Best Actor for Torch Song Trilogy in 1983.
Savion Glover revolutionized Broadway tap dancing with his raw, percussive style in Bring in 'da Noise, Bring in 'da Funk, which he also choreographed.
Lin-Manuel Miranda performed freestyle raps at the White House in 2009 mentioning Alexander Hamilton; the footage went viral and is considered the birth of the Hamilton concept.
The term 'musical comedy' evolved into 'musical play' in the 1940s as shows like Oklahoma! and Carousel integrated serious dramatic content with song and dance.
The megamusical era of the 1980s, dominated by Andrew Lloyd Webber and Cameron Mackintosh, featured spectacle-driven productions with massive sets and international tours.
The jukebox musical genre, which uses pre-existing pop songs to tell a story, includes shows like Mamma Mia!, Jersey Boys, Moulin Rouge!, and & Juliet.
Revival productions have become increasingly important on Broadway, with shows like Chicago, Cabaret, and Company proving that fresh interpretations can find new audiences.
The concept musical, pioneered by shows like Company and A Chorus Line, organizes itself around a theme rather than a traditional linear narrative.
The rock musical emerged in the late 1960s with Hair and has evolved through Rent, Spring Awakening, American Idiot, and Hadestown.
Broadway grossed over $1.8 billion in the 2018–2019 season, the highest in its history, with total attendance exceeding 14.7 million.
Broadway ticket prices have risen sharply; the average ticket cost in the 2022–2023 season was over $120, compared to about $35 in the 1980s.
Premium and VIP seating has become a significant revenue source, with top tickets for shows like Hamilton and Wicked selling for over $800 at face value.
The Actors' Equity Association, founded in 1913, is the labor union representing professional actors and stage managers in the United States.
An Equity card, showing membership in the Actors' Equity Association, is essential for performers working in professional Broadway, Off-Broadway, and touring productions.
Broadway understudies are unsung heroes of the theater; they must learn multiple tracks and be ready to perform on a moment's notice without additional rehearsal.
The swing role in a Broadway musical means learning multiple ensemble tracks and filling in for any absent cast member, often with very little notice.
Stage door interactions, where fans wait after shows to meet performers and get autographs, have been a beloved Broadway tradition for over a century.
Playbills, the free programs given to every Broadway audience member, have been published since 1884 and are iconic collectibles for theatergoers.
The phrase 'dark night' on Broadway refers to a scheduled evening when a theater has no performance; most Broadway theaters are dark on Mondays.
Wednesday matinees became a Broadway standard in the mid-20th century, joining Saturday matinees to give audiences two chances per week to see afternoon shows.
The original cast recording of My Fair Lady, released in 1956, was the best-selling album in the United States for years and popularized the cast album format.
The Hamilton Mixtape, released in 2016, featured covers and remixes of Hamilton songs by artists like Usher, Kelly Clarkson, Ja Rule, and Chance the Rapper.
Wicked became the highest-grossing show on Broadway on a weekly basis multiple times, regularly earning over $3 million per week during peak seasons.
The original Broadway production of Grease starred Barry Bostwick as Danny and Carole Demas as Sandy, though the 1978 film version with John Travolta is better known.
Stephen Sondheim won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama for Sunday in the Park with George in 1985, one of only a handful of musicals to receive the honor.
Lin-Manuel Miranda's Hamilton won the 2016 Pulitzer Prize for Drama, making Miranda one of the few musical theater writers to receive both a Pulitzer and multiple Tonys.
A Chorus Line won the Pulitzer Prize in 1976; the show was developed from taped conversations with real Broadway dancers about their lives and struggles.
Rent won the 1996 Pulitzer Prize for Drama, and Jonathan Larson was posthumously awarded the prize after his sudden death the night before the show's first preview.
South Pacific won the 1950 Pulitzer Prize for Drama, making Rodgers, Hammerstein, and Joshua Logan the first musical theater team to receive the honor.
Of Thee I Sing, the Gershwins' 1931 political satire, was the first musical ever to win the Pulitzer Prize for Drama.
Both parts of Angels in America won the Tony for Best Play in consecutive years (1993 and 1994), an unprecedented achievement for a single theatrical work.
The Tony Award for Best Musical has gone to some surprise winners, including A Gentleman's Guide to Love & Murder in 2014, which beat out Aladdin and Beautiful.
The 1968 Tony Awards were delayed for several days following the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. on April 4, 1968.
In 2015, Fun Home won the Tony for Best Musical, becoming the first show with a lesbian protagonist to receive Broadway's highest honor.
The Phantom of the Opera won seven Tony Awards in 1988 but lost Best Actor to Michael Crawford's co-nominee — wait, Crawford actually won for Phantom.
Michael Crawford's portrayal of the Phantom in the original Broadway cast of The Phantom of the Opera set the standard for every subsequent actor in the role.
Colm Wilkinson originated the role of Jean Valjean in both the London and Broadway productions of Les Misérables, and his interpretation remains definitive for many fans.
Jonathan Groff originated the role of King George III in Hamilton, stealing scenes with the hilariously imperious 'You'll Be Back,' despite having limited stage time.
Daveed Diggs won the Tony for Best Featured Actor in a Musical for Hamilton, playing the dual roles of Marquis de Lafayette and Thomas Jefferson.
Renée Elise Goldsberry won the Tony for Best Featured Actress in a Musical for her portrayal of Angelica Schuyler in Hamilton.
Leslie Odom Jr. won the 2016 Tony for Best Actor in a Musical for Hamilton, beating out his co-star Lin-Manuel Miranda in one of the ceremony's most talked-about results.
Sutton Foster has won two Tony Awards for Best Actress in a Musical: for Thoroughly Modern Millie in 2002 and Anything Goes in 2011.
Norbert Leo Butz won back-to-back Tony Awards for Best Actor in a Musical for Dirty Rotten Scoundrels in 2005 and Catch Me If You Can — actually, he won for Dirty Rotten Scoundrels.
Brian Stokes Mitchell is known as the 'Last Leading Man' on Broadway and won the Tony for Kiss Me, Kate's 1999 revival.
Donna Murphy won the Tony for Best Actress in a Musical in consecutive years for Passion in 1994 and The King and I in 1996.
Boyd Gaines is the only person to win Tony Awards in all four acting categories: Featured Actor (Play), Actor (Play), Featured Actor (Musical), and Actor (Musical).
The original cast of West Side Story in 1957 included Larry Kert as Tony and Carol Lawrence as Maria, with choreography by Jerome Robbins.
Barbra Streisand's only leading Broadway role was Fanny Brice in Funny Girl in 1964; she left the show after two years and never returned to a Broadway stage.
The 42nd Street Development Project in the 1990s transformed Times Square from a seedy area into the family-friendly tourist destination and theater hub it is today.
The New Amsterdam Theatre on 42nd Street was rescued from decay by Disney in the 1990s and became the home of The Lion King.
Studio 54, the legendary nightclub, was converted into a Broadway theater and has hosted shows including Cabaret, Spring Awakening, and American Buffalo.
The Richard Rodgers Theatre, originally named the 46th Street Theatre, is where Hamilton has been playing since it opened in August 2015.
The Lyceum Theatre, opened in 1903, is the oldest continuously operating legitimate theater on Broadway and is a designated New York City Landmark.
The Belasco Theatre was built by producer David Belasco in 1907 and is said to be haunted by his ghost, who reportedly appears in the balcony during performances.
Theater superstitions include never whistling backstage (it once signaled stagehand cues and could cause a scenery drop) and always leaving a light on in an empty theater.
The Tony Awards added the category of Best Revival of a Musical in 1994 and Best Revival of a Play in 1994 as well, recognizing the growing importance of revivals on Broadway.
The Drama Desk Awards, unlike the Tonys, honor both Broadway and Off-Broadway productions and are voted on by theater critics, journalists, and editors.
The Outer Critics Circle Awards recognize outstanding achievement in Broadway, Off-Broadway, and Off-Off-Broadway theater, as voted by critics from out-of-town publications.
The Lucille Lortel Awards honor outstanding Off-Broadway productions and are named after the legendary theater owner and producer.
Broadway shows typically have eight performances per week: evening shows Tuesday through Saturday, plus matinees on Wednesday, Saturday, and Sunday.
A Broadway tryout or preview period allows shows to make changes before officially opening; some shows have had months of previews, like Spider-Man: Turn Off the Dark with 182.
Spider-Man: Turn Off the Dark holds the record for the most expensive Broadway production ever, costing approximately $75 million and plagued by technical difficulties and injuries.
The 'Phantom Phenomenon' made Andrew Lloyd Webber the most commercially successful composer in Broadway history, with Phantom grossing over $1.3 billion on Broadway.
Cats introduced the concept of immersive environmental staging to Broadway, with the entire theater transformed into an oversized junkyard for Eliot's feline characters.
The notorious Broadway flop Carrie: The Musical, based on Stephen King's novel, closed after just five performances in 1988, losing its entire $8 million investment.
Moose Murders, which opened and closed on Broadway on the same night in 1983, is considered the worst Broadway play of all time by many critics.
Taboo, Boy George's musical about the 1980s London club scene, produced by Rosie O'Donnell on Broadway, lost approximately $10 million when it closed in 2004 after 100 performances.
The original Broadway cast of Hair in 1968 made headlines when cast members appeared nude at the end of Act One, a first for a Broadway musical.
Pippin's original 1972 production featured Bob Fosse's direction and John Rubinstein in the title role, while the 2013 revival reimagined the show in a circus setting.
The Drowsy Chaperone is a musical-within-a-comedy, narrated by a character called Man in Chair who plays his favorite cast album and comments on the action.
A Strange Loop by Michael R. Jackson won both the 2020 Pulitzer Prize for Drama and the 2022 Tony for Best Musical, exploring the inner life of a Black queer writer.
The 2022 revival of Company, originally planned for 2020, gender-swapped the lead role of Bobby to Bobbie, played by Katrina Lenk, earning five Tony Awards.
Stephen Sondheim died on November 26, 2021, at age 91, and Broadway dimmed its lights in his honor; he is widely regarded as the greatest lyricist-composer in Broadway history.
Jonathan Larson won the Tony for Best Musical, Best Book, and Best Original Score for Rent in 1996 — all posthumously, as he never saw his show performed.

Composer Jeanine Tesori has written scores for Fun Home, Caroline or Change, Shrek the Musical, Thoroughly Modern Millie, and Kimberly Akimbo, making her one of Broadway's most prolific female composers.
Kimberly Akimbo won the 2023 Tony for Best Musical, with Victoria Clark winning Best Actress for her portrayal of a teenager with a rapid aging condition.
The 1996 revival of Chicago, directed by Walter Bobbie and choreographed by Ann Reinking in the style of Bob Fosse, has become the longest-running American musical on Broadway.
Bebe Neuwirth won the Tony for Best Featured Actress in a Musical for the 1986 Sweet Charity revival, later becoming famous as the velvet-voiced Velma Kelly in the Chicago revival.
Gwen Verdon, Bob Fosse's muse and wife, originated roles in Damn Yankees, Sweet Charity, and Chicago and won four Tony Awards for Best Actress in a Musical.
Bob Fosse is the only person to win an Oscar, an Emmy, and a Tony in the same year — 1973 — for Cabaret (film), Liza with a Z (TV), and Pippin (Broadway).
The Tony Honors for Excellence in Theatre recognize individuals and organizations that have made extraordinary contributions to Broadway without fitting traditional award categories.
The Tony Award for Best Choreography has been won by legends including Jerome Robbins, Bob Fosse, Michael Bennett, Tommy Tune, Susan Stroman, and Andy Blankenbuehler.
Tommy Tune won 10 Tony Awards across performances, choreography, and direction, making him one of the most honored individuals in Tony history.
The first Tony Award ceremony had no competitive voting; awards were presented to recognize excellence in the 1946–47 Broadway season.
Hugh Jackman has hosted the Tony Awards multiple times and won a Tony for Best Actor in a Musical for The Boy from Oz in 2004.
Neil Patrick Harris hosted the Tony Awards four times and became known for his elaborate opening numbers that celebrated Broadway's diversity and energy.
The 2016 Tony Awards ceremony, hosted by James Corden, took place the same day as the Pulse nightclub shooting in Orlando, casting a somber tone over the celebration.
The Rodgers and Hammerstein partnership produced five of the most beloved musicals in history: Oklahoma!, Carousel, South Pacific, The King and I, and The Sound of Music.
Cole Porter's musicals include Anything Goes, Kiss Me Kate, and Can-Can, and his witty, urbane lyrics set the standard for sophistication in musical theater.
George Gershwin composed Porgy and Bess, An American in Paris, and many popular songs, blending classical, jazz, and popular music in groundbreaking ways.
Leonard Bernstein composed the scores for West Side Story, On the Town, Wonderful Town, and Candide, bringing symphonic complexity to the Broadway stage.
Jerry Herman wrote the scores for Hello, Dolly!, Mame, and La Cage aux Folles, known for his upbeat, hummable melodies and celebratory spirit.
Jason Robert Brown composed The Last Five Years, Parade, and The Bridges of Madison County, earning Tony Awards and establishing himself as a leading contemporary theater composer.
Maury Yeston composed the scores for Nine and Titanic, both of which won the Tony for Best Musical, and he wrote Grand Hotel.
Jonathan Tunick is Broadway's most celebrated orchestrator, winning multiple Tony Awards and an Oscar, and has orchestrated nearly all of Stephen Sondheim's musicals.
The original Guys and Dolls in 1950 starred Robert Alda (Alan Alda's father) as Sky Masterson and Vivian Blaine as Miss Adelaide.
Fiddler on the Roof's original 1964 production was directed and choreographed by Jerome Robbins, who created the iconic 'Bottle Dance' at Tzeitel's wedding.
The Wiz, an all-Black retelling of The Wizard of Oz with a Motown-inspired score, won the 1975 Tony for Best Musical.
La Cage aux Folles, the 1983 musical about a gay couple in Saint-Tropez, was groundbreaking for its positive portrayal of a same-sex relationship on Broadway.
Falsettos, William Finn's musical about a gay man navigating family dynamics during the AIDS crisis, received a acclaimed 2016 revival starring Christian Borle and Andrew Rannells.
The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee features audience participation, with real audience members invited on stage to compete in a spelling bee alongside the actors.
Matilda The Musical, based on Roald Dahl's novel, won four Olivier Awards in London before transferring to Broadway in 2013 and winning four Tony Awards.
Once on This Island, Lynn Ahrens and Stephen Flaherty's Caribbean-set musical, received a Tony-winning 2017 revival that transformed the Circle in the Square Theatre into a tropical setting.
Ragtime, set at the turn of the 20th century, weaves together three storylines involving a Black musician, a Jewish immigrant, and a WASP family in New Rochelle.
Hamilton's staging uses a turntable built into the stage floor, designed by David Korins, allowing characters and scenes to literally revolve around each other.
The original Broadway cast of The Lion King included Samuel E. Wright as Mufasa and Max Casella as Timon, and the show has been running continuously since 1997.
Les Misérables' famous barricade set piece is physically constructed onstage during the show, symbolizing the 1832 June Rebellion in Paris.
Wicked has been performed in over 100 cities in 16 countries and has been translated into languages including Japanese, German, Korean, and Dutch.
A Gentleman's Guide to Love and Murder features one actor playing eight members of the D'Ysquith family, all of whom must be murdered by the protagonist.
The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-Time uses the National Theatre's innovative design where the walls, ceiling, and floor of the set all become projection surfaces.
Next to Normal was the eighth musical in history to win the Pulitzer Prize for Drama, following in the footsteps of South Pacific, A Chorus Line, and Rent.
The Music Man's 'Ya Got Trouble' is a rapid-fire patter song that Robert Preston delivered without pausing for breath, establishing the con man Harold Hill's character.
Carousel's 'Soliloquy,' a nearly eight-minute solo for Billy Bigelow, is one of the longest and most demanding songs in the musical theater canon.
Kiss Me, Kate's backstage setting — where a theatrical couple fights during a production of The Taming of the Shrew — established the template for countless backstage musicals.
Porgy and Bess was considered a commercial failure when it premiered in 1935 but is now recognized as one of the greatest American operas ever written.
42nd Street's famous tap-dancing finale features the entire cast in a massive production number that recreates the spectacle of 1930s Busby Berkeley movie musicals.
Dreamgirls' 'And I Am Telling You I'm Not Going' is one of Broadway's most powerful showstoppers; Jennifer Holliday's original performance is considered definitive.
Evita was the first British musical to premiere in London's West End before transferring to Broadway, reversing the traditional direction of transatlantic transfers.
Jesus Christ Superstar was banned in South Africa during apartheid for its portrayal of a non-white Jesus, highlighting the political power of musical theater.
Hair's original Broadway company included Diane Keaton, who appeared in the show before launching her film career.
The Tony Lifetime Achievement Award has been given to theater luminaries including Harold Prince, Stephen Sondheim, Carol Burnett, James Earl Jones, and Angela Lansbury.
Rodgers and Hart, the songwriting team before Rodgers partnered with Hammerstein, wrote classics like Pal Joey, Babes in Arms, and The Boys from Syracuse.
Cy Coleman composed Sweet Charity, The Will Rogers Follies, City of Angels, and Barnum, covering an extraordinary range of musical styles from jazz to circus.
Charles Strouse composed the music for Annie, Bye Bye Birdie, and Applause, creating some of Broadway's most memorable and hummable scores.
Frank Loesser wrote both music and lyrics for Guys and Dolls, The Most Happy Fella, and How to Succeed in Business Without Really Trying, winning the Pulitzer for the latter.
How to Succeed in Business Without Really Trying won both the 1962 Tony for Best Musical and the Pulitzer Prize for Drama.
Damn Yankees, about a fan who sells his soul to the devil to help the Washington Senators beat the Yankees, featured Gwen Verdon in her Tony-winning star-making role.
The Pajama Game, directed by George Abbott and Jerome Robbins, won the 1955 Tony for Best Musical and launched the career of choreographer Bob Fosse.
Bye Bye Birdie, inspired by Elvis Presley's draft into the Army, won the 1961 Tony for Best Musical and starred Dick Van Dyke and Chita Rivera.
Man of La Mancha, the musical adaptation of Don Quixote, introduced 'The Impossible Dream,' which became a standard covered by countless artists.
Oliver!, Lionel Bart's musical adaptation of Charles Dickens' Oliver Twist, was the first British musical to win the Tony for Best Musical in 1963.
Cabaret has had two major Broadway revivals — in 1987 and 1998 — with the 1998 Sam Mendes/Rob Marshall production at Studio 54 becoming a sensation starring Alan Cumming.
Alan Cumming's portrayal of the Emcee in the 1998 Cabaret revival was a radical reimagining that added androgynous sexuality and menace to the character.
Fosse, the 1999 revue celebrating the choreography of Bob Fosse, won the Tony for Best Musical, beating out three original musicals.
Contact, which won the 2000 Tony for Best Musical despite having no original score and using only pre-recorded music, sparked controversy about what qualifies as a musical.
The Broadway League, formerly the League of American Theatres and Producers, is the national trade association for the commercial theater industry in North America.
Off-Broadway has served as a proving ground for shows that later transferred to Broadway, including Hamilton, Little Shop of Horrors, and Rent.
Little Shop of Horrors, based on Roger Corman's 1960 B-movie, ran for five years Off-Broadway and features the man-eating plant Audrey II.
The Public Theater, founded by Joseph Papp, has been instrumental in developing new works for Broadway, including A Chorus Line, Hamilton, and Fun Home.
Shakespeare in the Park, produced by The Public Theater in Central Park's Delacorte Theater, has offered free performances since 1962 and features major Broadway stars.
Playwrights Horizons, Manhattan Theatre Club, and Roundabout Theatre Company are major nonprofit theater companies that regularly develop and produce work that transfers to Broadway.
The Roundabout Theatre Company is the largest nonprofit theater company in the United States and operates multiple Broadway and Off-Broadway venues.
Broadway's record-breaking week occurred during the holiday season of December 2019, when shows grossed over $50 million in a single week.
The Hamilton film, shot with the original Broadway cast and released on Disney+ in July 2020, was watched by an estimated 2.7 million households in its first ten days.
Wicked's 2024 film adaptation, directed by Jon M. Chu, starred Cynthia Erivo as Elphaba and Ariana Grande as Glinda and became a massive box office hit.
Les Misérables' 2012 film adaptation featured live singing on set rather than lip-syncing to pre-recorded tracks, a technique championed by director Tom Hooper.
Chicago's 2002 film, directed by Rob Marshall, won six Academy Awards including Best Picture, reviving interest in movie musicals.
The Phantom of the Opera's 2004 film adaptation was directed by Joel Schumacher and starred Gerard Butler and Emmy Rossum, though it received mixed reviews.
Grease's 1978 film starring John Travolta and Olivia Newton-John became the highest-grossing musical film of all time and spawned the hits 'Summer Nights' and 'You're the One That I Want.'
The Sound of Music's 1965 film won five Academy Awards including Best Picture, and its soundtrack became the best-selling album of 1965 in the United States.
West Side Story has been filmed twice: the 1961 version won 10 Academy Awards, and Steven Spielberg directed a 2021 remake that won Ariana DeBose the Oscar.
My Fair Lady's 1964 film starred Audrey Hepburn as Eliza Doolittle (with Marni Nixon dubbing her singing) and Rex Harrison reprising his Tony-winning role.
The Tony Awards' In Memoriam segment each year honors theater artists who have passed away, often accompanied by a live musical performance.
The 73rd Tony Awards in 2019 featured a memorable opening number by host James Corden that culminated in a spectacular Broadway medley.
The Tony Awards are voted on by approximately 700 members of the Broadway community, including actors, directors, producers, and theater journalists.
The Tony Administration Committee determines eligibility for Tony nominations, and their decisions about what qualifies as a 'new' musical versus a 'revival' can be controversial.
Phantom of the Opera's 'Music of the Night' is one of the most recognized songs in musical theater, a seductive ballad where the Phantom reveals his underground lair to Christine.
Les Misérables' 'One Day More' is an Act One finale that weaves together seven different character themes into a single powerful ensemble number.
Hamilton's 'My Shot' establishes Alexander Hamilton's relentless ambition with hip-hop wordplay that includes the double meaning of 'shot' as both opportunity and bullet.
Wicked's 'Popular' is a comedic showpiece where Glinda attempts to make Elphaba socially acceptable through a makeover, contrasting with the dramatic 'Defying Gravity.'
The Sound of Music's 'My Favorite Things' has become a jazz standard, most famously recorded by John Coltrane, far beyond its original theatrical context.
South Pacific's 'Bali Ha'i' creates an atmosphere of exotic mystery and longing and was written by Rodgers and Hammerstein to represent the allure of an island paradise.
Fiddler on the Roof's 'If I Were a Rich Man' features Tevye's comic fantasy about wealth, with its distinctive nonsense syllables and klezmer-influenced melody.
Company's 'The Ladies Who Lunch' is a bitter, boozy showstopper originally performed by Elaine Stritch, who turned it into one of Broadway's most iconic musical moments.
Chicago's 'Cell Block Tango' features six murderesses each explaining their crimes with the repeated refrain 'He had it coming,' becoming one of Broadway's most famous ensemble numbers.
Dreamgirls was inspired by the Motown era, and its musical style ranges from 1960s soul and R&B to 1970s disco, charting the evolution of Black popular music.
The Lion King's puppet designs by Julie Taymor draw on Indonesian, Japanese, and African theatrical traditions, creating a visual style unlike anything previously seen on Broadway.
Rent's 'La Vie Bohème' is an extended celebration of bohemian life, name-dropping artists, writers, and cultural figures while the characters toast to their unconventional lives.
Cabaret's 'Maybe This Time' was added for the 1972 film and later incorporated into stage revivals; it became one of Liza Minnelli's signature songs.
Into the Woods' first act ends with everyone getting their wishes, while the second act devastatingly explores the consequences — Sondheim's statement on the complexity of happy endings.
The Broadway season traditionally runs from late spring to late spring, with the Tony Awards eligibility cutoff determining which shows compete each year.
Since the 1990s, Disney Theatrical Productions has become one of Broadway's most powerful producers, with hits including The Lion King, Aladdin, Frozen, and Mary Poppins.
Juilliard, NYU Tisch, Carnegie Mellon, University of Michigan, and University of Cincinnati CCM are among the top training programs for aspiring Broadway performers.
The 'triple threat' — a performer who can act, sing, and dance at a high level — remains the gold standard for Broadway musical performers.
The Shubert brothers (Sam, Lee, and Jacob) built or acquired over 100 theaters across the United States in the early 20th century, creating a theatrical empire.
David Belasco, a legendary early 20th century producer and director, was known for his realistic staging effects, such as using real food and actual sunlight on stage.
The Ziegfeld Theatre, built in 1927 for Florenz Ziegfeld, was one of Broadway's most opulent venues before it was demolished in 1966.
The Mark Hellinger Theatre, now the Times Square Church, hosted My Fair Lady's original run and is one of Broadway's great lost theaters.
Broadway theaters were originally located in lower Manhattan; they gradually moved uptown, reaching the current Times Square area by the early 1900s.
The marquee of a Broadway theater is its most visible advertising; the art of designing show logos and marquee images is a specialized field within theatrical marketing.
Broadway stagehands, represented by IATSE Local One, are among the highest-paid union workers in America, reflecting the technical complexity of modern Broadway productions.
Hair's 'Aquarius/Let the Sunshine In' became a No. 1 pop hit for The 5th Dimension in 1969, spending six weeks atop the Billboard Hot 100.
Godspell's 'Day by Day' reached No. 13 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1972, making it one of the most commercially successful Broadway songs of its era.
The American Theatre Wing, co-founded by Antoinette Perry, administers the Tony Awards and also runs programs including the Theatre Intern Network.
The Tony Award for Best Direction of a Musical has been won by theater giants including Harold Prince (eight times), Jerome Robbins, Michael Bennett, and most recently, Marianne Elliott.
Michael Bennett conceived, directed, and choreographed A Chorus Line, drawing on real dancers' stories in groundbreaking taped interview sessions.
Patti LuPone's 2008 Tony-winning performance in Gypsy saw her deliver the song 'Rose's Turn' with such ferocity that her final note held the audience in stunned silence.
The Tonys have honored non-traditional works including puppet shows (Avenue Q), dance pieces (Contact), and concerts (Springsteen on Broadway as a special Tony event).
Bruce Springsteen performed a 236-show solo run at the Walter Kerr Theatre from 2017 to 2018 with Springsteen on Broadway, earning a special Tony Award.
The original Broadway production of West Side Story did not win the Tony for Best Musical in 1958; that honor went to The Music Man.
Gypsy has never won the Tony for Best Musical in any of its four Broadway productions, despite being widely considered one of the greatest musicals ever written.
Hamilton tickets were so in-demand that they created a secondary market frenzy, with resale prices reaching $10,000 or more in the months after the show opened.
Lin-Manuel Miranda performed his final Broadway performance as Hamilton on July 9, 2016, with celebrity-studded audiences for his final shows.
The 'Hamilton Lottery' offered $10 front-row tickets through a digital lottery, making the sold-out show accessible to fans who couldn't afford premium prices.
The Broadway debut of Nicole Scherzinger in Sunset Boulevard in 2024 earned rave reviews and a Tony Award, demonstrating Broadway's continued ability to attract pop stars.
Sunset Boulevard, Andrew Lloyd Webber's musical about a faded silent-film star, originally starred Glenn Close, who won the Tony for her imperious portrayal of Norma Desmond in 1995.
The 2024 revival of Sunset Boulevard, directed by Jamie Lloyd, used a stripped-down staging with handheld cameras projecting live close-ups of the actors onto screens.
Audra McDonald is the only performer to win Tony Awards in all four acting categories: Featured Actress Play, Featured Actress Musical, Actress Play, and Actress Musical.
Hugh Jackman returned to Broadway in 2022 as Harold Hill in The Music Man revival, earning a Tony nomination and drawing massive audiences.
Daniel Radcliffe won the Tony for Best Featured Actor in a Musical for Merrily We Roll Along in 2024, decades after his film fame as Harry Potter.
Merrily We Roll Along, originally a Sondheim flop in 1981 that closed after 16 performances, finally received a triumphant Broadway revival in 2023 that won the Tony for Best Revival.
Jonathan Bailey, known for Bridgerton, made his Broadway debut in the 2024 Merrily We Roll Along revival, winning rave reviews as Charley Kringas.
The Outsiders won the 2024 Tony for Best Musical, based on S.E. Hinton's classic novel, featuring a score by Jamestown Revival and Justin Levine.
Stereophonic, a play about a rock band recording an album in the 1970s inspired by Fleetwood Mac, won five Tony Awards in 2024 including Best Play.
The Imperial Theatre on West 45th Street has been home to iconic productions including Les Misérables, Dreamgirls, and Ain't Too Proud.
The Broadhurst Theatre has hosted premieres of plays by Arthur Miller, Tennessee Williams, and August Wilson, making it one of Broadway's most storied dramatic houses.
The tradition of standing ovations on Broadway has become nearly universal, with audiences rising to their feet at virtually every performance regardless of quality.
Cast recordings are a vital revenue stream and marketing tool for Broadway shows; the Hamilton cast album is the best-selling cast recording of the 21st century.
The phrase 'What a show!' engraved on the Tony Award medallion was suggested by the award's designer and encapsulates the spirit of Broadway's highest honor.
Ethel Merman's opening-night performance of 'I Got Rhythm' in Girl Crazy in 1930 is considered the moment that established the Broadway belt voice as a theatrical instrument.
Mary Martin's Peter Pan, first broadcast live on NBC in 1955, was watched by an estimated 65 million viewers and became a touchstone of American television and theater.
Broadway's first racially integrated audience occurred at the premiere of Show Boat in 1927, when producer Florenz Ziegfeld invited Black audience members to sit in the orchestra section.

The Federal Theatre Project, part of the New Deal, employed thousands of theater workers during the Great Depression and produced groundbreaking works including an all-Black Macbeth directed by Orson Welles.
Eugene O'Neill's Long Day's Journey Into Night is considered the greatest American play by many critics and won the Pulitzer Prize posthumously in 1957.
Tennessee Williams won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama twice: for A Streetcar Named Desire in 1948 and Cat on a Hot Tin Roof in 1955.
Arthur Miller's Death of a Salesman premiered with Lee J. Cobb as Willy Loman; the role has since been played by Dustin Hoffman, Brian Dennehy, and Philip Seymour Hoffman.
Edward Albee won three Pulitzer Prizes for Drama: for A Delicate Balance (1967), Seascape (1975), and Three Tall Women (1994).
August Wilson's Fences and The Piano Lesson both won Pulitzer Prizes, and his ten-play Pittsburgh Cycle is considered one of the greatest achievements in American drama.
Tracy Letts won the 2008 Pulitzer Prize for August: Osage County, a sprawling family drama that also won five Tony Awards.
Lynn Nottage is the only woman to win the Pulitzer Prize for Drama twice, for Ruined in 2009 and Sweat in 2017.
The tradition of opening night parties on Broadway is a hallmark of the industry, with producers hosting lavish celebrations at venues like Sardi's while waiting for reviews.
Sardi's restaurant on West 44th Street has been Broadway's unofficial clubhouse since the 1920s, famous for its caricatures of theater celebrities on the walls.
The 'Sardi's caricature' — having your likeness drawn and hung on the restaurant's walls — is considered a mark of Broadway stardom and has honored hundreds of theater legends.
Broadway shows traditionally open on Thursday nights to give critics time to write reviews for the Sunday papers, though this convention has become less rigid in recent years.
The New York Times theater critic has historically been Broadway's most powerful reviewer; a negative Times review could close a show, while a rave could ensure a long run.
Frank Rich, chief theater critic of the New York Times from 1980 to 1993, was nicknamed 'The Butcher of Broadway' for his influence over which shows survived.
Brooks Atkinson, the legendary New York Times theater critic, has a Broadway theater named after him — the Brooks Atkinson Theatre on West 47th Street.
The Tony Awards ceremony has been held at Radio City Music Hall, the Beacon Theatre, and other New York City venues, but most frequently at Radio City.
The youngest Tony winner ever is Frankie Michaels, who won Best Featured Actor in a Musical for Mame in 1966 at age 10.
The oldest Tony winner for a performance was Roger Rees, though Cicely Tyson won the Tony for Best Actress in a Play for A Trip to Bountiful at age 88 in 2013.
The Tony for Best Original Score recognizes music and lyrics written specifically for a show, excluding jukebox musicals that use pre-existing songs.
The Tony for Best Book of a Musical recognizes the non-musical script; some brilliant scores have been let down by weak books, and vice versa.
Broadway's grosses are publicly reported weekly by the Broadway League, allowing fans and industry observers to track the financial health of every show.
The COVID-19 pandemic forced all Broadway theaters to close on March 12, 2020; when they reopened in September 2021, shows implemented vaccination requirements for audiences.
The first Broadway show to reopen after the COVID shutdown was Springsteen on Broadway, followed by Pass Over and Hadestown.
Waitress star Sara Bareilles stepped into the lead role of Jenna multiple times during the show's run, making her one of the few featured songwriters to star in their own musical.
Tina: The Tina Turner Musical chronicles the life of the legendary singer and ran on Broadway from 2019 to 2024, with Adrienne Warren winning the Tony for Best Actress.
Dear Evan Hansen's marketing campaign was one of the most innovative in Broadway history, using social media to build a grassroots fan base before the show even opened.
Beetlejuice developed a massive TikTok following among young fans who created viral trends around the show's songs, helping drive ticket sales and contributing to its return.
The Great Comet of 1812, starring Josh Groban, featured immersive staging at the Imperial Theatre with performers moving through the audience during an electro-pop retelling of Tolstoy.
Natasha, Pierre & The Great Comet of 1812 began as a small Off-Broadway show in a tent in the Meatpacking District before transferring to Broadway.
Ain't Too Proud: The Life and Times of the Temptations won the Tony for Best Choreography in 2019 for Sergio Trujillo's dynamic Motown-inspired dance sequences.
The Prom, about a group of Broadway actors who travel to Indiana to support a gay student banned from her prom, was adapted into a 2020 Netflix film directed by Ryan Murphy.
Jagged Little Pill, based on Alanis Morissette's album, won the 2021 Tony for Best Book and addressed themes of addiction, sexual assault, and racial identity.
Broadway Cares/Equity Fights AIDS is the theater community's primary fundraising organization, raising over $300 million since 1988 through audience appeals and special events.
The Easter Bonnet Competition, organized by Broadway Cares, features creative bonnets and performances by Broadway casts and crews to raise money for AIDS-related causes.
The Gypsy of the Year competition, also by Broadway Cares, features Broadway company members performing original skits and numbers to raise funds — now called the Broadway Backwards event.
The Phantom of the Opera's original Broadway cast included Michael Crawford as the Phantom, Sarah Brightman as Christine, and Steve Barton as Raoul.
The longest-running play in Broadway history is The Phantom of the Opera — but as a musical; the longest-running play is The Mousetrap by Agatha Christie in London, not Broadway.
Broadway's highest-grossing musical of all time in total revenue is The Lion King, which has earned over $1.7 billion in Broadway ticket sales alone.
Hamilton's opening night on Broadway was August 6, 2015, at the Richard Rodgers Theatre; the show had previously played at The Public Theater Off-Broadway.
The Public Theater's production of Hamilton at the Newman Theater in early 2015 was so successful that tickets were impossible to get even before its Broadway transfer.
Wicked's original Broadway cast included Idina Menzel as Elphaba, Kristin Chenoweth as Glinda, Joel Grey as the Wizard, and Norbert Leo Butz as Fiyero.
The Broadway musical Parade, about the wrongful conviction and lynching of Leo Frank, was revived in 2023 to widespread acclaim and won the Tony for Best Revival of a Musical.
Sweeney Todd's 2023 Broadway revival starred Josh Groban and Annaleigh Ashford, winning the Tony for Best Revival and introducing the show to a new generation.
Some Like It Hot, the musical based on the classic Billy Wilder film, opened on Broadway in 2022 and won the Tony for Best Choreography for Casey Nicholaw's work.
Alex Newell made history in 2023 by winning the Tony for Best Featured Actor in a Musical for Shucked, becoming the first nonbinary actor to win a Tony.
Shucked, a country-themed musical comedy about corn farming, was the surprise hit of the 2023 Broadway season and earned several Tony nominations.
Broadway has increasingly embraced diverse casting, with shows like Hamilton, Six, and The Wiz leading the way in centering performers of color in leading roles.
The tradition of color-conscious and color-blind casting has evolved significantly since the 1990s, with Broadway now regularly casting actors regardless of race in classic roles.
Broadway's economic impact on New York City is estimated at over $15 billion annually when accounting for restaurants, hotels, transportation, and other tourism-related spending.
Approximately 65% of Broadway tickets are purchased by tourists from outside the New York metropolitan area, making Broadway a major driver of New York City tourism.
